stimulus: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) has rapidly become the most powerful infrared observatory ever launched, producing landmark images of distant galaxies and exoplanet atmospheres; even so, only a small fraction of its observing time is allotted to objects within our own solar system. Astrophysicist Pablo Restrepo and colleagues argue that a smaller, dedicated infrared telescope optimized for solar-system targets would allow continuous monitoring of icy moons and a comprehensive survey of trans-Neptunian objects. Restrepo and colleagues' recommendation therefore implies that the JWST ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: will likely be reconfigured so that its observing time is devoted mainly to solar-system bodies once the dedicated telescope is launched.
is incapable of producing useful infrared observations of trans-Neptunian objects without significant hardware upgrades.
will most likely continue to focus its observing time on targets beyond the solar system.
has already provided enough data on icy moons to make additional infrared observations of them unnecessary.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The text states that only a small fraction of JWST's observing time is allotted to solar-system objects, and that researchers are arguing for a separate, dedicated telescope to handle that work. The clearest implication is that the JWST will continue to be used primarily for objects beyond the solar system, leaving solar-system work to the proposed new instrument.

Choice A is incorrect: nothing in the text suggests JWST will be reconfigured; the argument is that a new telescope is needed precisely because JWST is occupied with other targets. Choice B is incorrect because the text does not claim JWST cannot observe trans-Neptunian objects — only that it does not devote much time to them. Choice D is incorrect: the researchers are calling for more, not less, observation of icy moons.
